message.content:
Trusted Relationship (T1199) involves adversaries exploiting existing connections between organizations and their trusted third-party providers to gain unauthorized access to target networks. These relationships often involve elevated access permissions granted to external providers such as IT services, managed security providers, and infrastructure contractors. Adversaries may compromise valid accounts or credentials used by these third parties to infiltrate internal systems, often bypassing standard security measures.

The potential impact includes unauthorized access to sensitive data, disruption of services, and further lateral movement within the victim's network. Common exploitation methods include compromising accounts at cloud solution partners, using legitimate access granted to managed service providers, and leveraging compromised certificates for authentication.

Mitigation strategies include implementing strict access controls and monitoring for third-party connections, regularly auditing and limiting the scope of third-party access, enforcing multi-factor authentication, and ensuring robust incident response plans are in place to quickly address any breaches involving trusted relationships. Regularly reviewing and updating security policies related to third-party access can also help minimize risks associated with this technique.
